book.cometcroft.field_guide.building.a_p: Furniture, roofs, windows — the cozy catalogue.

This pack carries thousands of building pieces beyond vanilla: real furniture, proper sloped roofs, window frames, garden fences, lamps and lanterns of every shape.\
\
The trick to browsing it all: type **@macaw** in the recipe list's search box and one maker's whole family appears at once. The **@** works for every mod name.
A place to sit is where a house starts. Every wood has its own chairs, tables, desks, drawers and wardrobes — same shapes, your timber.
Flat stair-roofs and glass squares did their best. Proper sloped roofs and framed windows do better.
Roofs come in lower, steep, attic and top pieces that meet cleanly at ridges and corners. Shutters swing, and there are blinds and curtain rods besides.
One little tool multiplies every block you already have.

Right-click with it to open the chiseling screen: drop in planks or stone and swap the stack between dozens of carved variants — beams, tiles, brick patterns. Left-click a placed block to restyle it where it stands.
When the croft looks the way you pictured it, stretch your legs. The **Further Afield** chapter covers waystones, packs and wings — ways to leave home and always find your way back.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.building.tim:
Break one log and the whole tree comes down, leaves and all. No floating trunks, no ladder of dirt.\
\
When you want just the one log — a treehouse, a trim, a beam — sneak while you chop and the tree stands.
Timber work goes best with a proper axe in hand. Iron is cheap once you've a vein or two banked, so keep a spare by the door.

Spot an ore, hold **middle mouse** while you break it, and the entire vein follows it out of the wall.\
\
Two small rules: you need the right tool for the ore, and there's a short breather between veins. Everything still costs its honest durability — bring a spare pickaxe for the big coal seams.

Feed the stonecutter your mined stone and pick the exact cut — stairs, slabs, bricks — one block in, one block out, nothing wasted. When the chests are heavy with logs and cut stone, read on: *A Prettier Croft* shows you what to make of them.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.description: A homestead under a strange sky. The chapters of croft life wait on the right — start with **Getting Started**. Wherever you wander in this book, the **X** in the corner steps you back a page, and back to this list.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.food.a_real_: From campfire to kitchen, one block at a time.

Three blocks turn cooking from a chore into a craft.\
\
The **cutting board** portions and preps ingredients — place one on it and use a knife. The **skillet** sears quick food right over the heat. And the **stove** is the campfire all grown up: flat, tidy, made for pans.
Set the skillet or the cooking pot on top, and you're a chef.
A knife on the board turns one ingredient into portions — cuts of meat, slices, and the occasional bonus.
Once the pantry grows, remembering what you *can* cook becomes the hard part. This block remembers for you.

The clever one. Put it beside your chests, counters, and fridge: it lists every recipe you can make *right now* from what's stored in them, and crafts it in one click. The huge dish roster stops being homework.
A **sink** pours endless water. An **oven** bakes. A **fridge** keeps ingredients at hand — and stops them aging (more in "Keeping It Fresh").
Like everything in the kitchen: hover it, press R, build it when you're ready. Then read **Keeping It Fresh** — the fridge is half of that story.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.food.eat_the:
The same meal every day gets old — literally. Eat one food over and over and it fills you a little less each time. Never below half; this is a nudge, not a punishment.\
\
Mix up your plate and everything stays hearty. The cure is the fun part: hover any ingredient and press R, and there is always a new dish to try.
Toss whatever fruit the season gives you into a bowl. Cheap, cheerful, and different from whatever you ate last.
Bread and eggs you'll have from day one. Sandwiches craft right in your inventory grid — no kitchen needed.
When the orchard comes in, bake something proper. A pie sits on the table and serves slices to the whole household.

The cooking pot is where variety really opens up — soups, stews, and dinners by the dozen. See **The Cooking Pot** at the start of this chapter, and press R on anything that looks tasty.\
\
One more habit: cook more than you can eat and it will spoil. **Keeping It Fresh**, next in this chapter, shows you where to shelve the surplus.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.food.keeping: Food ages. The pantry has answers.

Food doesn't keep forever here. Over about a week of in-game days it slowly turns, and its tooltip shows how fresh it still is.\
\
It's gentle on purpose — a week is a long time, and nothing rots in your hands mid-adventure. But a proper stockpile wants a plan.

The **fridge** stops time: nothing spoils inside it, ever.\
\
**Cabinets, counters, and baskets** slow aging to a quarter speed — a pantry shelf holds food for close to a month.\
\
Pockets and plain chests do nothing at all. So the rhythm is: cook what you'll eat today, shelve what you'll eat this season, freeze the hoard.
The cold heart of the larder. Comes out white, but a splash of dye in the crafting grid recolours it to suit the kitchen.
The pantry pieces. Every wood has its own cabinet, and the basket sits happily on any shelf. Counters from the kitchen chapter slow spoilage just the same.
With the larder sorted, food stops being a worry. There's one more thing a body needs: a drink. **Water and Warmth** covers the water bar and how to keep it topped up.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.food.name: Food & Kitchen
book.cometcroft.field_guide.food.the_coo: Build one early. Simmered meals feed you far better than raw crops, and variety keeps you strong.

Set the pot over a campfire or another heat source, right-click it, and lay in your ingredients.\
\
Pot recipes don't fit on these pages, and they don't need to: **hover any dish and press R** to see exactly what goes into it. That works right here in this book, and everywhere else.
Carrot, potato, beetroot and something leafy. Hover it and press R for the full recipe.
No pot needed for this one, just a crafting grid: a baked potato, cooked beef, and milk. Once the pot is simmering, read on to *A Real Kitchen* — a proper room makes all of this much easier.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.food.the_keg:
Every good homestead ends up with a cellar, and every good cellar ends up with a keg.\
\
Grow **hops**, brew ales, cider, stouts and stranger things — each glass carries its own small comfort.
Any wood's slabs and planks will do, but the keg wants a **hop** at its heart — grow those first. Mugs come four at a time; you'll want the spares.
A hop crafts straight into seeds, so one good harvest keeps the hop garden going on its own.
Wheat seeds, toasted in the furnace. Most brews ask for these alongside hops, so keep a jar of them by the keg.

Each brew is a keg recipe of its own — hops, dry seeds, and something that gives it character. And remember the hops in your garden answer to the year like any other crop; the **Seasons** chapter picks up that thread.
One of many. Hover any brew and press R to see what the keg wants for it.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.food.the_see: Every seed in the world, for an emerald.
A kitchen is only as good as its garden, and the garden is only as good as its seeds.
Place it and a merchant moves in. They trade emeralds for seeds, saplings, and fertilizer — by far the fastest way to grow the crop collection your cooking will ask for.
The market sells the seeds; these keep them growing.

Fill it up and sprinkle the beds to hurry your crops along. The market sells three grades of fertilizer too, and each is craftable — hover one and press R. And once the garden is thriving, some of the harvest deserves a keg — the next page shows the cellar's side of the kitchen.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.food.water_a:
Beside your hunger sits a water bar. It drains slowly — a day around the croft barely dents it — but hot places and long roads will.\
\
Any water helps. For a cleaner drink, boil a bottle over a campfire — or hover **purified water** and press R for the other ways of making it.
Fill it at any pond and sip as you walk. One of the best early crafts in the pack.
Set a bottle of water over the fire and wait. A furnace or smoker does the same job, and there's a craftable **water filter** too — hover it and press R.

Deserts scorch, peaks freeze, and getting soaked in winter is as miserable as it sounds. It's a nudge rather than a threat — dress for the trip, keep a fire near in the cold, and dry off under a roof.\
\
Cold weather isn't forever, either — and a warm meal helps. Keeping the garden stocked for one is the next page's business: the seed market.
For knowing exactly how the air feels before you blame the weather.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.further_afie: Elytra, the friendlier way.
Earning Your Wings
Here, the ender dragon drops a pair of elytra when it falls. Beat the dragon and the wings are yours — no hunting end cities for them.
Wings
The dragon's parting gift, and the last mode of travel you'll ever need.
The elytra
Your wings sit in their own trinket slot — you'll find it beside your armor in the inventory — so you keep wearing your chestplate while you fly.
Flying comfortably
Paper and gunpowder make a rocket; set one off mid-glide for a push. More gunpowder in the craft means a stronger one.
Firework rocket

Wings needn't stay plain. At a smithing table you can trim them like armor, and a dye in the crafting grid changes their color.\
\
If a landing ever goes wrong, don't worry — the next entry, **If You Fall**, explains why that's an errand, not a disaster.
Making them yours
Maps, waypoints, and a compass that answers questions.
Finding Your Way

The minimap rides in the corner of your screen, and **M** unfolds the full map of everywhere you've been. You can pin waypoints on it, and they'll hang on the horizon in the world until you arrive.\
\
Death leaves its own marker, too — between that and your grave, nothing is ever really lost.
Maps

When the map can only show you where you've been, this shows you where to go next. Ask it for any structure — a village, a tower, a temple, a ruin — and it points the way there. The wilds of this pack are unusually full of places worth pointing at.
The explorer's compass

Use it in hand to pick a structure, then follow the needle. When you find somewhere worth keeping, attune to a nearby waystone and home is never far. That's the world walked — the last chapter, **Better Together**, is about sharing it.
Explorer's Compass
Death is an errand, not a disaster.
If You Fall

When you die, your things don't scatter and burn. They wait in a grave right where you fell — safe from other players, with your experience inside too.\
\
Walk back, claim it, carry on. If the way back is long, **/back** will carry you straight to where you fell.\
\
Before you wander far, it's worth typing **/sethome** at the croft — then **/home** brings you back from anywhere, alive or otherwise.
Graves

One more mercy for explorers: treasure chests in ruins and dungeons hold separate loot for each player. Your friend opening a chest first takes nothing from you — everyone finds their own.\
\
Which is a good reason to bring a friend along. And to wander with fewer falls in the first place, the next page covers maps, waypoints, and a compass that answers questions.
Shared ruins
Further Afield
A backpack with a bed rolled up in it.
Pack Your Bag
One bag for the long road: extra storage, two tanks for liquids, and a rolled-up sleeping bag.
The traveler's backpack
Hover it and press R for the recipe — and there's a whole family of fancier packs beyond this one.
Home on your back
You'll need one of these for the backpack itself. Any wool color works, and a finished bag can be re-dyed.
The tanks won't fill themselves — a hose moves liquids in and out of the pack.

The pack rides on your back without taking your armor. When night catches you far from home, set it down and unroll the sleeping bag — morning comes wherever you are.\
\
Once your bag is packed, the road is open. And when walking starts to feel slow, the next page is about earning your wings.
Sleeping out
The fast way home.
Waystones

Old standing stones wait in villages and out in the wilds. Use one to attune to it — from then on, any waystone can carry you to any other you've visited, for a pinch of experience that grows with the distance.\
\
Craft one for the croft, and home is never more than the nearest stone away.
Waystones
Every stone variant works the same, so pick the one that suits the build.
A one-use trip to any waystone you know, from wherever you happen to be standing — and it asks no experience at all.
The reusable cousin. It wears down a little with each trip, but it will carry you home for a long while.
With the way home settled, pack for the way out — the next page covers the traveler's backpack, a bag with a bed rolled up in it.
Before you go
book.cometcroft.field_guide.getting_star: The few buttons this pack adds to your hands.
Keys Worth Knowing

**R** shows how the hovered item is made. **U** shows what it's used for. These work everywhere — including in this book.\
\
The panel beside your inventory lists every item in the pack, with a search box under it. Type a name to find a thing, or **@** plus a mod name — like **@macaw** — to browse one maker's whole range.\
\
Hovering a shulker box shows what's packed inside without placing it.
In your inventory

**Chopping:** break one log and the whole tree falls — sneak to take just the one. **Mining:** hold **middle mouse** on an ore and the whole vein follows. *Timber and Stone*, in the Building chapter, has the finer points of both.\
\
**Looking around:** a spare mouse button (or **F6**) zooms your view. **M** unfolds the world map, and the minimap always rides in the corner of your screen.
Out in the world

Nothing here needs practising — the keys will find your fingers soon enough. Try **R** on the next item that puzzles you. And when your chests begin to overflow, turn to **A Tidy Croft** for drawers and one screen that holds the lot.
Where next
Getting Started
Drawers for the big piles, one screen for the rest.
A Tidy Croft

Chests fill up fast on a farm. Two helpers keep the clutter down.\
\
**Drawers** hold a big pile of one single thing — potatoes, wheat, cobblestone. Right-click the front to put in, punch it to take out.\
\
**The storage terminal** shows everything in all your chests and drawers on one screen, and lets you take from any of them.
Where things go
There are double and quad versions for smaller piles — hover them and press R.
Place it touching your chests and drawers. It quietly links everything around it into one network.
Put the terminal against the connector and open it. Every chest in the network, one screen. That's the croft tidy — next stop is the **Food & Kitchen** chapter, where the harvest meets the pot.
What this place is, and how this book works.
Welcome to the Croft

A croft is a small homestead. This one is yours.\
\
This is a slower, warmer kind of Minecraft. Real seasons turn the year, kitchens actually matter, and the sky over the croft is stranger than it first looks.\
\
If you're new, start with **Your First Days** — a short checklist that carries you through the first evenings. Everything else in this book waits until you need it: one chapter for each part of life out here.
Welcome

Two keys do most of the teaching in this pack, and they work here in this book and in every inventory screen:\
\
Hover any item and press **R** to see how it's made. Press **U** to see what it's used for.\
\
That means every dish, tool, and block you'll ever meet carries its own instructions with it. When in doubt: hover it, press R. This book is for the things a recipe can't tell you.
Using this book

One thing worth knowing about this book: the **X** in the corner doesn't just close it — it steps you *back*. From a guide it returns you to the chapter, and from a chapter to the list of all chapters.\
\
Closing the book some other way remembers your place, and you'll reopen right where you left off. So if the book ever seems small, press that X — the rest of it is one step up.
Finding your way around
A gentle checklist for the first week.
Your First Days

The basics, in order:\
\
Chop a tree — here, the whole thing comes down at once. Sneak while chopping if you only want the one log. Raise a small shelter before dark and craft a **bed**.\
\
Then get a fire going and cook whatever the day gave you. Even a simple campfire meal beats eating things raw.
Day one
Any colour of wool will do. Sleep skips the night and sets where you'll wake.
Dinner, light, and a warm doorstep — one block does all three.

Food is the heart of this pack, so start early: a **cooking pot** and a **cutting board** are your first two kitchen tools. The Food & Kitchen chapter carries on from there.\
\
Plant everything you find, too — but hover the seeds first to check which season they like.
Day two — the kitchen begins

Craft a **calendar** so the seasons never surprise you, and a **leather flask** so the road doesn't dry you out. The **Seasons** chapter covers the first, and **Food & Kitchen** the second.\
\
After that, follow your nose. Build out the kitchen, clear the fields, watch the sky for falling rocks. Each of those has its own chapter waiting.
Day three and onward
book.cometcroft.field_guide.name: Comet Croft Field Guide
book.cometcroft.field_guide.seasons.farm: What to plant when, and what a greenhouse is for.

Hover any seed and its tooltip lists the seasons it likes.\
\
Planting out of season isn't a disaster here. The crop just grows much slower — but it will never wither. Nothing you plant can be lost to the calendar.

Glass changes everything. A crop with any glass block within sixteen blocks directly above it grows as if it were always the right season.\
\
A glass roof over the garden turns winter into spring. Deep underground works too — below height 48, crops ignore the seasons entirely.

Smelt sand in a furnace and roof the beds. One clear layer overhead, and every season is growing season underneath. Build it before the first snow and the kitchen never goes quiet. If a new season calls for seed you don't have, the market in **Food & Kitchen** sells every kind for emeralds. And the seasons aren't the only thing this sky does — **The Strange Sky**, next, is about the rest.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.seasons.name: Seasons
book.cometcroft.field_guide.seasons.the_: Spring, summer, autumn, winter — and how to keep track.

The Croft has a real year. Leaves turn, the rain comes and goes, and in winter the world goes white and the ponds freeze over.\
\
Seasons pass on their own as the days go by — six days each, in Early, Mid and Late stretches. You'll notice it in the grass and the trees first, and the current season sits just under your minimap.
Hang one by the door. It shows the season and the day, so you always know what's coming.

For the tinkerers. This block gives off a redstone signal that climbs as its season passes; use it with an empty hand to pick which season it watches. When you're ready to plant something, read on — **Farming with the Seasons** covers what grows when.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.the_sky.mete: Meteors, craters, and what they're worth.

This sky drops things. Now and then a meteor streaks down and lands somewhere nearby, going off with a bang and leaving a crater behind.\
\
Finding the crater is worth the walk. The meteoric rock there breaks into chunks you won't find in any mine — mine it without Silk Touch and gather them up. A chunk on its own crafts into a pile of raw iron, or you can save them for better things.
Chunks and iron ingots, mixed. Alloy is what turns a basic laser into an advanced one — hover it and press U to see where it leads.
Every laser needs a set of these. Use a spare pair on a placed laser and it will show you the area it watches over.

For when the falling rocks start to feel less like a gift and more like a problem. Place it near the croft and it will shoot incoming meteors out of the sky before they land. The biggest ones need the advanced laser.
Meteors aren't the only thing worth watching for up there. On a clear night, take a spyglass out — the next page is about the stars.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.the_sky.name: The Strange Sky
book.cometcroft.field_guide.the_sky.star: Constellations, planets, and naming your own.

On a clear night, look up through a spyglass. The stars out here are real: constellations wheel overhead through the year, and the brighter wanderers among them are planets.\
\
Best of all, you can trace new constellations yourself and give them names. The sky remembers them.
Two copper ingots topped with an amethyst shard.
Amethyst grows inside hollow geodes underground. Watch cave walls for patches of smooth basalt and calcite — crack through and the purple is inside. The clusters regrow, so one geode is plenty.

Zoom in with the spyglass, then press your pick-block key (middle click) to switch it between modes. In constellation mode, hold left click and drag lines from star to star. In star mode you can select a single star or planet instead.\
\
Once something is selected, type **/sga:name** followed by a name to christen it, or **/sga:info** to learn a little about it.

Everyone on the server sees the same stars, but the constellations you draw start out yours alone. Select one and type **/sga:share** to get a code a friend can use to add it to their own sky.\
\
When you've had your fill of the stars, keep an eye on the moon. Some nights it rises changed — the next page, **Strange Moons**, covers that.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.the_sky.stra: Some nights, the moon rises changed.

Most nights the moon is just the moon. Perhaps one night in twenty it rises changed: blood-red, pale white, swollen huge, or dark as an eclipse.\
\
Each one bends the night in its own way — some make the dark more dangerous, some more generous. If the sky looks wrong at sundown, step out carefully, or watch from a window with something warm.\
\
None of them will keep you from your bed. If you'd rather not meet one, sleep it away — every strange moon lets you.
There's a croft to build under all this weather. The **Building the Croft** chapter is next — timber, stone, and the prettier things.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.together.cla:
Land here can be claimed, chunk by chunk. Inside your claim, only you can build, open doors, and touch chests — the croft stays exactly as you left it.\
\
The easiest way: press **M** for the world map and right-click to claim or unclaim the chunks under your cursor. Claims show up as colored overlays on the map. Or press **'** (apostrophe) for the claims screen wherever you are.

Building with someone? Make a party and your claims are shared with its members:\
\
**/openpac-parties create**, then **/openpac-parties member invite (name)** — and they answer with **/openpac-parties join (party name)**.\
\
Once that's done, walk the edges of your croft with the map open and claim the chunks it sits on. Then build without worrying.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.together.han: Homes, teleports, and finding your way back.

**/sethome** marks the spot where you stand — you can keep up to five homes. **/home** brings you back to one.\
\
**/tpa (name)** asks to teleport to a friend — they have thirty seconds to accept, then you appear beside them.\
\
**/back** returns you to wherever you just were — including where you died.\
\
**/trigger nv** switches on night vision, for when a cave is too dark to enjoy. Type it again to switch it off.

**/rtp** throws you somewhere wild and far away, for when you feel like starting a long walk home.\
\
When you find a spot worth keeping, set a home there and read on — the next page shows you how to claim the land. And waystones, over in **Further Afield**, will spare you the walk back.
book.cometcroft.field_guide.tooltip: A homestead under a strange sky.
